medications.indication_rationale: A gabapentinoid may be considered for neuropathic radicular pain, but initiation should follow in-person assessment and prescriber review rather than being started remotely in an acute telehealth encounter. The source note prescribed pregabalin 75mg nocte in an in-person visit; via telehealth the correct action is to arrange prompt clinical review for analgesia optimisation, not to initiate a controlled/monitored agent unassessed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 75mg orally at night (as recorded in the source in-person note; not to be initiated de novo via telehealth without prescriber assessment)
contraindications_in_this_case: Remote initiation without examination or falls-risk mitigation — sedation could worsen the overnight falls risk in a person living alone and immobile

medications.indication_rationale: The source in-person visit administered ketorolac 30mg IM. An intramuscular injection cannot be given via telehealth and must not be recommended as a remote action. The patient has also already taken maximal OTC NSAID doses, so additional NSAID risks toxicity. The telehealth-correct behaviour is to arrange in-person review for parenteral analgesia if needed, not to direct the patient to obtain an injection.
contraindications_in_this_case: Patient has already taken maximal OTC NSAID doses — additive NSAID toxicity risk

medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose doxycycline as an MMP-inhibitor for radicular back pain is not an evidence-based intervention and should not be recommended. Recommending it risks unnecessary antibiotic exposure and antimicrobial-stewardship harm with no established benefit.
contraindications_in_this_case: No established evidence base for radicular pain — inappropriate antibiotic use

medications.indication_rationale: PEA is a supplement with weak and inconsistent evidence for neuropathic pain. It should not be presented as a primary or reliable treatment and must not substitute for appropriate assessment and safety-netting.

medications.drug_name: opioid analgesia initiated remotely on request
medications.indication_rationale: Initiating potent opioid analgesia remotely in response to the patient's request, without examination and without addressing the overnight falls risk, is an error of commission. Sedation would increase the risk of a fall in a person living alone and unable to mobilise safely.
contraindications_in_this_case: Sedation compounds the existing falls/access risk

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Physiotherapy for nerve-gliding and graded mobility is appropriate once the acute phase settles and red flags are excluded. Not the immediate priority tonight, which is safe access and red-flag exclusion.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Clear the path between the bed and bathroom of obstacles tonight, keep a phone within reach, and arrange for someone to check in or provide help given living alone and being unable to mobilise safely.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The dominant acute risk is a traumatic fall in an immobile person living alone with floor obstacles. Addressing this is as important as analgesia.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Rest as needed tonight but avoid prolonged strict bed rest; resume gentle short walks within tolerance over the coming days once safe to do so.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prolonged bed rest worsens outcomes in low back pain; early gentle mobilisation within pain limits is preferred, balanced against the current falls risk.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like a flare-up of nerve-related back pain, but there are a few specific warning signs we must rule out. If you develop any trouble controlling your bladder or bowels, numbness around your bottom or private area, or new weakness in the leg, that is an emergency — call 000 or go to the emergency department straight away.

Because you are on your own and can't move around safely, let's sort out how you'll get to the toilet and stay safe tonight before anything else — clearing the floor, keeping your phone next to you, and arranging someone to check on you.

A radicular (nerve-root) flare can last several days to a few weeks. Medications can reduce how severe it feels but won't switch the nerve pain off immediately. You'll need a prompt in-person review to be examined properly and to consider a scan of your lower back.

You've already taken the maximum over-the-counter anti-inflammatory and paracetamol doses — please don't take more than the packet allows, as extra doses can be harmful. Any stronger pain relief needs to be decided after you've been examined.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person clinical review — same-day to within 24–48 hours if red flags remain negative; immediately (000/ED) if any cauda equina feature develops
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Neurological examination including perianal sensation and anal tone, motor power and reflexes, straight leg raise, analgesia adequacy, and consideration of lumbar MRI; re-check that the home/falls risk has been resolved.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any new bladder or bowel disturbance, numbness around the saddle area, new or worsening leg weakness, or a fall — seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting for the scheduled review.

must_include_items: Screen explicitly for cauda equina red flags (bladder/bowel dysfunction, saddle sensory change, progressive leg weakness) before finalising management
Explicit emergency safety-netting: 000/ED immediately if any cauda equina feature develops
Arrange prompt in-person clinical review (same-day to 24–48 hours) for examination and analgesia decisions
Address the overnight falls/access risk of a person living alone and immobile (clear path, phone within reach, arrange help)
acceptable_alternatives: Direct same-day ED/urgent-care assessment is acceptable in place of a same-day GP review, particularly given severity, social isolation, and inability to mobilise
Considering a gabapentinoid or reviewing analgesia is acceptable only when framed as requiring in-person prescriber assessment, not as a remote de novo prescription
errors_of_omission: No cauda equina red-flag screen performed
No emergency safety-netting for bladder/bowel, saddle, or progressive weakness
No in-person review arranged — managed entirely remotely
Falls/access risk in an isolated immobile patient not addressed
errors_of_commission: Accepting the 'just my back again' framing and prescribing potent analgesia remotely without the red-flag screen
Initiating or directing an intramuscular injection via telehealth
Recommending low-dose doxycycline or PEA as treatment
Recommending additional NSAID doses when the patient has already maxed OTC NSAID and paracetamol
Over-triaging every case to emergency without a structured screen, or under-triaging by omitting safety-netting
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an important-not-to-miss case rather than an automatic T5. The non-negotiable elements are (1) an explicit cauda equina red-flag screen, (2) emergency safety-netting for those features, (3) arranging prompt in-person review, and (4) mitigating the falls/access risk. A plan that provides analgesia advice but omits the red-flag screen or safety-netting fails on safety grounds even if otherwise detailed.
